text: How satisfied are you with the way democracy is working in India?
notes: Topline Q2. Total satisfied: 72%. One of Pew's highest globally.

text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of Prime Minister Narendra Modi?
notes: Topline Q4a. Total favorable: 79%. Strong ideological signal — BJP vs. opposition split.

text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP)?
Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Indian National Congress (INC / Congress party)?
notes: Topline Q5b. Total favorable: 60%. Lower than BJP (73%) — tests political lean differentiation.

text: Do you think India's influence in the world is getting stronger, weaker, or staying about the same?
notes: Topline Q14g. Strong consensus but tests national pride dimension.

text: Having a representative democracy where citizens vote for representatives who then make laws and policies — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
notes: Topline Q42b. Tests democratic values vs. authoritarian tendency.

text: Having a strong leader who does not have to bother with parliament or elections — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
notes: Topline Q42c. India among highest globally on support for strong leader (up significantly vs. 2017). Key test of BJP vs. opposition persona differentiation.

text: How would you describe the current economic situation in India?
notes: Topline Q5, Spring 2017. Distribution normalized to exclude DK=5%. Note: 2024 data shows India among top countries for positive economic views globally — this direction likely holds or strengthened.

text: How much do you trust the national government of India to do what is right for the country?
notes: Topline Q4, Spring 2017. Distribution normalized to exclude DK=4%. Total trust: 89%.

text: When children today in India grow up, do you think they will be better off or worse off financially than their parents?
notes: Q6, Spring 2018. Distribution normalized to exclude DK=12%. Very optimistic — tests economic hopefulness by region/income.

text: How important is religion in your life?
notes: Religion survey 2021, fieldwork Nov 2019–Mar 2020. Highly skewed — India among most religious countries globally. Tests religious identity anchoring.

text: How much do you agree or disagree: A wife must always obey her husband.
source: Pew Research Gender Roles in India, 2022 (N=29,999)
notes: Gender survey 2022, fieldwork 2019–20. Total agree: 87%. Highly conservative on traditional gender norms. Tests gender attitudes differentiation by education/urban tier.

text: When jobs are scarce, men should have more right to a job than women. Do you agree or disagree?
source: Pew Research Gender Roles in India, 2022 (N=29,999)
notes: Gender survey 2022. Total agree: 80%. Distribution of completely/somewhat agree estimated from report totals. Tests education and urban/rural differentiation.

text: How important is it that women have the same rights as men in India?
source: Pew Research Gender Roles in India, 2022 (N=29,999)
notes: Gender survey 2022. Highly skewed but tests whether personas can hold both in12 (wife obeys) and in14 (women have equal rights) simultaneously — a real feature of Indian public opinion.

text: How much of a threat is global climate change to India?
notes: Topline Q17d, Spring 2017. Distribution normalized to exclude DK=25%. Note: Spring 2024 data shows 89% say climate change is serious threat (58% very serious), suggesting A is likely higher with current data. 2017 used for consistent published topline distribution.
